Automatic Refinement of Force Fields Based on Phase Diagrams Bin Jin, Bin Han, Wei Feng, Kuang Yu,* and Shenzhen Xu* Cite This: https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.jctc.5c02032

Downloaded via KYOTO UNIV on April 17, 2026 at 01:29:59 (UTC). See https://pubs.acs.org/sharingguidelines for options on how to legitimately share published articles.

* Supporting Information sı

ABSTRACT: Exact characterization of phase transitions requires sufficient configurational sampling, necessitating efficient and accurate potential energy surfaces. Molecular force fields with computational efficiency and physical interpretability are desirable but challenging to refine for complex interactions. To address this, we propose a force field refinement strategy with phase diagrams as top-down optimization targets based on automatic differentiation. Using gas−liquid coexistence as a paradigm, we employ an enhanced sampling technique and design a differentiable loss function to evaluate force fields’ depiction of phase diagrams. The refined force fields produce gas−liquid phase diagrams matching well with targets for two modeling systems, moreover, phase-equilibrium chemical potentials are also automatically determined during the refinement process, which confirms our approach as an effective automated force field development framework for phase transition studies.

1. INTRODUCTION Phase transitions play a fundamental role in the realms of thermodynamics and dynamics.1−5 They occur in regions exhibiting intense fluctuations within a system and represent intrinsic properties of materials. Study and application of phase transitions underpin critical advancements in materials science, energy storage technologies, and condensed matter physics.6−12 Computational simulations provide a powerful tool for investigating phase transitions.13−17 However, sufficient sampling of the configurational space is required to accurately describe the process involving different phases. Let us take the simulation of gas−liquid phase coexistence as an example, we may encounter a few challenges regarding the critical point. When the temperature is far from the critical point, fluctuations of physical quantities are relatively small, while the transformation between different phases during the configurational sampling is infrequent, thus requiring a large number of steps to achieve sufficient sampling. Conversely, close to the critical point, the transformation between different phases is frequent in the sampling process, but fluctuations of physical quantities become relatively large. The paralleltempering Monte Carlo (PTMC) method combines the advantages of sampling under these different thermodynamic conditions to resolve the above challenges.18−20 Regardless of the sampling methods, an accurate and efficient potential energy surface (PES) is the foundation. There are three common types of PES surrogate models: the classical molecular force field, the machine learning force field (MLFF), and the ab initio calculation method. While ab initio calculations are accurate, they are computationally expensive for large-scale simulations. Training an MLFF capable of © XXXX American Chemical Society

accurately describing the configurational space of interest also presents challenges, which requires sufficient representative samples that may be encountered in the practical simulations to be included in the training data set to prevent potential failures during application of MLFF, and the computational cost during the training process substantially increases with the training data set size for systems with diverse components and complicated interactions. Additionally, the complexity of the MLFF architecture and the number of parameters may be scaled with the system, which also impacts the inference cost. By contrast, the advantages of classical molecular force fields include a high computational efficiency and an inherent physical interpretability of the terms involved in mathematical expressions. Typically, the classical force field can be optimized through both bottom-up and top-down strategies. To obtain classical molecular force fields for the research on materials’ phase transitions, experimental macroscopic phase-equilibrium properties can be selected as the top-down optimization targets. In this work, we consider the gas−liquid phase transitions with the commonly used equilibrium density− temperature (ρ-T) phase diagram (Figure 1), which exhibits the phases’ coexistence behavior. Several studies21−24 have incorporated thermophysical quantities including saturated gas and liquid densities, vapor pressure, and enthalpy of vaporReceived: December 6, 2025 Revised: April 9, 2026 Accepted: April 9, 2026

[Page 2]

In the top-down strategy of the automatic differentiation technique, the experimental physical quantities of interest are represented by the differentiable ensemble averages from simulations. When applying this strategy to refine a reliable force field which could be employed to investigate materials’ phase transitions, successful construction of a thermodynamic phase diagram (usually benchmarked with experimental measurements) and accurate prediction of phase-equilibrium states are prerequisites. However, for the gas−liquid phase transitions, recent work using automatic differentiation only took the liquid density and evaporation enthalpies as the topdown optimization.32 Overall direct determination of phaseequilibrium states at phase transition was not achieved in the aforementioned force field refinement work only using discrete saturated gas and liquid densities. In this study, we aim to develop a systematic approach to refine molecular force fields automatically using the DMFF platform, based on preacquired phase diagrams, and select the density distribution to capture the phase-equilibrium state and extract two phase densities simultaneously during the simulation. Some studies employ distribution of physical quantities such as radial distribution function,36 membrane lateral density profile37 as optimization targets. However, a direct comparison between simulated and reference distributions at discrete points can cause the optimization to overemphasize certain regions of the distribution. To overcome this limitation, the Kullback− Leibler divergence38 offers a continuous measure of similarity between two distributions, which have been used in the package MDRefine39 implementing the refinement of the ensemble, the force field and the forward model. Different density distributions of the related thermodynamic conditions from the phase diagrams can be simulated with multiple trajectories simultaneously using the PTMC method. The DMFF platform performs analysis of multiple simulation trajectories under different thermodynamic conditions using the Multistate Bennett Acceptance Ratio (MBAR) algorithm.40 We highlight that the MBAR reweighting technique is also perfectly suitable for our employed PTMC sampling method with multiple-trajectory simulations. This work demonstrates the validity and practicality of our proposed approach for automatic refinement of force fields with the gas−liquid phase diagrams as the optimization object.

Figure 1. Scheme of the gas−liquid phase diagram and the hyperparallel tempering Monte Carlo (HPTMC) method. The blue and violet curves denote the gas and liquid phases, respectively. The gray solid point denotes the critical point. Boxes with particles illustrate the structures of phases across different temperatures and associated chemical potentials. The green, yellow and red arrows denote the coexistence of the gas and liquid phases in different replicas. More arrows indicate more frequent transformation between gas and liquid phases during statistical sampling. The hollow black arrows denote the replica exchange.

ization to enhance the performance of classical force fields in describing phase transitions. Given the highly dimensional force field parameter space, some of these works21,23,24 have employed machine learning techniques like Gaussian process regression and support vector machine to establish mappings between force field parameters and target properties, reducing the computational costs associated with evaluating physical quantities for specific force field parameter sets via simulation. However, more complicated expressions with more parameters need to be incorporated in the classical molecular force field to depict complex interactions in various materials’ systems more accurately, and the transferability between different systems thus becomes questionable. Too many parameters lead to difficulty in refining a force field model through direct parameter space search without the guidance of the gradient of the objective function with respect to force field parameters or higher-order information. Regarding this issue, the automatic differentiation technique has enabled efficient and automatic gradient computation and benefited force field parameter refinement, underpinning several packages built based on different platforms, including TorchMD,25 JAXMD,26 SPONGE,27 chemtrain,28,29 and the Differentiable Molecular Force Field (DMFF).30 Different from end-to-end differentiable MD engines like TorchMD and JAX-MD, DMFF and DiffTRe31 implemented in chemtrain avoid the gradient vanishing/exploding issues and excessive memory demands in end-to-end differentiation through the entire MD trajectory. Meanwhile, DMFF is applicable for various force field function forms and multiple types of object functions for different physical properties (density, evaporation enthalpy, gas uptake, transport coefficients, spectroscopy and so on).32−34 In addition to the classical molecular force field, it can also be extended to the refinement of MLFF, which intrinsically features a large number of parameters.33,35

2. METHODS 2.1. Hyper-Parallel Tempering Monte Carlo As for the gas−liquid phase equilibration, the grand canonical ensemble (GCE) is more suitable than the isothermal−isobaric NPT ensemble, since the volume fluctuation would be too large in the sampling of gas−liquid phases’ coexistence. We can simulate the GCE by the grand canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC) method.41−43 Combining the PTMC approach with the GCMC sampling, the hyper-parallel tempering Monte Carlo (HPTMC) method44,45 was proposed in earlier work to improve the sampling efficiency of the GCE (Figure 1). The basic principle of the HPTMC method is introduced briefly as follows. For a particle-number variable system, the GCE probability density of a state

2.3. Differentiable Loss Function The cornerstone of the refinement process lies in the construction of a loss function. The basic idea of our loss function design is (1) defining a phase-equilibrium density distribution as the reference, which involves information on gas and liquid densities from the target phase diagram; (2) maximizing the similarity between the reference density distribution and the reweighted density distribution (obtained via the MBAR approach) by adjusting the parameters included in the loss function. Given M independent trajectories under different ensemble conditions (sampling ensembles) from the HPTMC simulation, each comprising Nr samples, the reweighted density distribution of the target ensemble k characterized by a chemical potential μk and an inverse temperature βk is expressed as

The HPTMC method integrates these two types of trial moves with a predetermined probability ratio.

Leveraging the thermodynamic ensembles involved in the phase diagrams sampled via the HPTMC method, we establish the workflow of the force field refinement as shown in Figure 2. Initial chemical

where ρrj is the particle density of the sample with the index j within the trajectory r, {θi} represents the set of all force field parameters, Wr,j(μk,βk, {θi}) denotes the weight of the corresponding sample for the target ensemble k. (refer to Supporting Information Subsection S4.4 for the approximation of the Dirac-δ function) Note that the chemical potentials {μk} of the target ensembles are also undergoing an optimization process in our refinement framework. We also have M target ensembles, and they share the same temperatures with the corresponding sampling ensembles. The refined chemical potentials {μk} will then be used for computing the sampling ensembles in the next loop of HPTMC resampling. This process enables efficient and accurate determination of phase-equilibrium chemical potentials simultaneously among different thermodynamic conditions, which is typically unfeasible by manual adjustment. In single-phase regions of either gas or liquid, where interparticle interactions could be treated as relatively short-ranged, the system can be assumed to be independent. Under such conditions, the distribution of an extensive thermodynamic quantity such as density typically exhibits a Gaussian-like form. As the system approaches phase coexistence, the distributions corresponding to the gas and liquid phases appear simultaneously. Therefore, the reference density distribution can be defined as a bimodal Gaussian distribution, representing the two density peaks associated with the gas phase and the liquid phase, respectively. The areas under two Gaussian peaks are set equal, which approximates a phase-equilibrium state. Figure 3. Structures of gas and liquid phases for the L-J potential system and the CO2 system (a), and the results of phase diagram optimizations with the force field refinement using the DMFF platform for the L-J potential system (b) and the CO2 system (c,d). The blue crosses represent the estimated gas and liquid densities of the initial force field from the HPTMC simulations with the nonequilibrium chemical potentials. The blue dashed lines and the red lines with circle points correspond to the computed phase coexistence curves using the HPTMC method with the initial force field and the refined force field under equilibrium chemical potentials, respectively. The standard deviations for all estimated phase densities are calculated based on 7 repeated optimization processes for the simulated phase diagram situation in panels (b) and (c) and 5 repeated optimization processes for the experimental phase diagram situation in panel (d). Temperatures to which the phase coexistence curves of the initial force fields do not extend are higher than the critical point. The green and violet lines denote the targeted coexistence curves, the green lines correspond to simulation results with preset force field parameters, and the violet lines are from experiments. The critical points of the CO2 system from the experiment and our simulations using the refined force field are shown in (d).

intramolecular interactions, extensively employed in the simulation across gaseous, liquid and solid materials. The CO2 system has more complicated interactions, with important applications around its critical point in target component extraction, polymer processing, refrigeration, supercritical drying and cleaning and so on.49 In the L-J potential system, all physical quantities are calculated with the dimensionless arbitrary units (a.u.) for simplicity. A periodic supercell with size 7 × 7 × 7 a.u.3 is employed with a potential truncation at 2.5 a.u.. Temperatures used for different replicas of the HPTMC simulations and the reweighted density distribution calculations are selected as 1.00, 1.05, 1.10, 1.13, 1.16 a.u.. (refer to Supporting Information Subsection S2.1 for the details of the force field setup and the sampling parameters used in HPTMC simulations). For the CO2 system, a periodic cell with size 20 × 20 × 20 Ang3 is considered by using the TraPPE force field.50 (refer to Supporting Information Subsection S2.2 for the TraPPE force field details and the sampling parameters used in HPTMC simulations) Temperatures for different replicas of the HPTMC simulations and the reweighted density distribution calculations are set as 270, 275, 280, 285, 290, 295, 300 K. To demonstrate our proposed idea of force field refinement, we first preselect a set of force field parameters for both of the L-J potential system and the CO2 system, and use the HPTMC method (the sampling efficiency of this sampling method and the convergence of two key HPTMC parameters have been validated as shown in Figures S4a,b and S5, S6, respectively) to generate the simulated target phase diagrams (the sampled density distributions are shown in

Since the weights are obtained by the MBAR algorithm, and due to the analytical form of the reference density distribution, this loss function is differentiable with respect to the refining parameters. If necessary, a penalty term restraining the widths’ difference between the density distribution peaks (representing the two distinct phases) can be added to the loss function. Refer to Supporting Information Subsection S3.3 for the details of the penalty term.

2.4. Computational Details Two systems, a Lennard-Jones (L-J) potential46−48 system, and a carbon dioxide (CO2) system (Figure 3a) are chosen to demonstrate the validity and efficiency of our proposed phase-diagram-based force field refinement strategy. The L-J potential is a classical model for

[Page 5]

Figure 4. Evolution of reweighted density distributions through the force field refinement process with the simulated phase diagram as the target for the L-J potential system under temperatures (a) 1.00 a.u., (b) 1.16 a.u. and for the CO2 system under temperatures (c) 270 K, (d) 295 K. The blue and red curves illustrate reweighted density distributions of the initial and the refined force fields, respectively. The reweighted density distribution is calculated by the MBAR method using the trajectories simulated by the HPTMC method. Relation between density fluctuations of two phases and isothermal compressibility is discussed in Supporting Information Subsection S4.6. Figure S4c,d. We then apply perturbations to the force field parameters, obtain the initial trajectories of the perturbed force fields with nonequilibrium chemical potentials, and employ our developed refinement framework to see if the parameters can be optimized toward the original values. The number of samples, optimization methods, learning rates, optimization steps and resampling interval used in the refinement process of the L-J potential system and the CO2 system can be referred to Supporting Information Section S3. We note that the preset force field parameters of the L-J potential system and the CO 2 system are obtained from previous literatures.45,50 For the CO2 system, in addition to the target ρ-T diagram generated by our simulations, we can also use the experimental CO2 gas−liquid phase diagram51 as a target to justify the effectiveness of our developed force field refinement approach.

bias when fitted with a bimodal Gaussian distribution. As the force field refinement calculations proceed, the chemical potentials will approach the values for phase-equilibrium, and the two-peak features of the density profiles also become more obvious, leading to better fitting with the bimodal Gaussian distribution. This trend can be reflected by the evolution of reweighted density distributions along the force field refinement process as shown in Figure 4. More detailed results of the evolution of reweighted density distributions under different thermodynamic conditions are presented in Figures S7−S9. After the force field refinement gets converged, the coexistence curves (red lines with circle points) computed with the refined force field match well with the targets as shown in Figure 3b−d. For the CO2 system with the experimental phase diagram as the target, our refinement procedure is indeed effective (as shown in Figure 3d, and Figure S9 for the evolution of reweighted density distribution during the refinement), although not achieving a quantitatively perfect match. We need to point out that the representational capacity of the TraPPE force field is constrained by its limited number of parameters, and the phase densities estimated by the bimodal Gaussian distribution may also exhibit certain numerical deviations from the true values. We observe that for all cases of refined force fields, the standard deviations of the estimated densities increase as the temperature approaches the critical point. This trend is consistent with the growing fluctuation amplitudes and diverging correlation lengths expected near the critical point. For refinements targeting the simulated phase diagrams, the refined force fields yield highly consistent coexistence curves across different runs. In contrast,

3. RESULTS We can see in Figure 3b−d that the initially predicted gas and liquid densities (blue crosses) by the perturbed force field before refinement deviate significantly from the target values (green or violet lines) across our studied temperature range. We also note that, these initial density points’ trend with respect to temperatures looks quite unsmooth, primarily due to two reasons: (1) the chemical potentials employed in the initial simulations are not yet optimized to achieve exact phaseequilibrium, causing errors for the positions of the density profile’s peaks compared to the truly equilibrium state; (2) phase densities are estimated by fitting the reweighted density distribution with a bimodal Gaussian distribution (refer to Supporting Information Subsection S4.5 for details of the fitting process). Consequently, when the system is relatively far from a phase-equilibrium state, the density distribution may not exhibit an obvious two-peak feature, which could lead to

[Page 6]

refinements targeting the experimental phase diagrams exhibit somewhat greater variability. This difference likely stems from the fact that the simulated phase diagrams can be exactly reproduced by force fields with appropriate parameters, leading to similar parameter optimization directions in different runs. The experimental phase diagrams, however, cannot be perfectly matched within the chosen functional form, resulting in multiple feasible parameter sets that minimize the loss function along different optimization paths. This interpretation is also supported by the similar refined force field parameter values obtained across independent optimization runs, as shown in Figure S10. To clarify the force field parameter refinement effects by disentangling the influence of parameter perturbation from that of nonequilibrium chemical potentials, we have performed additional optimization processes in which the chemical potentials were adjusted while the force field parameters were fixed to yield a converged phase diagram for the initial force field. We can see in Figure 3b−d that phase coexistence curves (blue dashed lines) of the initial force fields are significantly different from the target curves (green or violet lines) for both systems, clearly demonstrating the necessity and effect of parameter refinement. In addition, we can also observe a dramatic distinction between the critical regions predicted by the initial force field and the refined force field. It is obvious in Figure 3b−d that the critical region of the initial force field with equilibrium chemical potentials lies lower in temperature for both systems. This difference in terms of the critical region prediction further supports the refinement effectiveness. We need to explain that the estimated gas and liquid densities (blue crosses) of the initial force fields with nonequilibrium chemical potentials in the critical region are merely fitting results without rigorous physical meanings. Since both the phase coexistence curves and the corresponding phase-equilibrium chemical potentials of the initial force fields deviate substantially from the target values, the overall parameter optimization may not be decoupled into two sequential processes, i.e., locating the phase-equilibrium state and refining force field parameters. In other words, the optimization of the force field parameters and the phaseequilibrium chemical potentials is better to proceed in a concerted manner, which precludes a manual stepwise approach. Our refinement strategy addresses this challenge by simultaneously optimizing both sets of variables, thereby guaranteeing this coupled process to be carried out automatically. We also see in Figure 3d that the critical point predicted with the refined force field is close to the experimental one. We need to clarify that the information on the experimental critical point is not included in our force field refinement calculations, this comparison thus indicates a certain transferability of the force field refined by our proposed workflow in terms of predicting the system’s thermodynamic properties. We note here that the computed critical-point temperature might be overestimated due to the strict criteria of identifying a continuous phase transition (refer to Supporting Information Subsection S4.2 for details) introduced by us to set up a quantitative standard. Besides, the transferability of the refined force fields for the CO2 system is also demonstrated by computing the pressure−temperature phase diagram and isobaric heat capacity (refer to Supporting Information Subsection S4.3 for details). Overall, the results demonstrate that our developed force field refinement strategy is applicable

to multiple material systems with different forms of force fields, including a toy model of L-J potential and a more practical modeling system of CO2 across the gas and liquid phases. The refinement methodology owns novelty residing in three key aspects. (1) Using the HPTMC method to enhance the sampling efficiency of the phases’ equilibration, which is perfectly suitable for the multiensemble MBAR reweighting technique employed in our work. (2) Using the automatic differentiation technique (commonly employed in the artificial intelligence nowadays) to automatically refine the force field, and efficiently determine the phase-equilibrium chemical potentials at different phase transition points. (3) Designing a differentiable loss function to evaluate the capability of a force field for reproducing a gas−liquid phase diagram.

4. DISCUSSION AND OUTLOOK In addition to the above results, several methodological insights merit further discussion. During the refinement process, the initially perturbed force field parameters are refined toward the target values effectively, and the sensitivity of the phase coexistence ρ-T curve on each parameter can be reflected by the parameters’ variation (shown in Figure S10). As for the CO2 system, refinement of the parameter σO is the most effective, while the other parameters remain relatively close to their initial values, indicating that the performance of the TraPPE force field on the gas−liquid phase diagram prediction most sensitively depends on the parameter σO, which possibly stems from the fact that oxygen atoms are located at the two ends of the linear CO2 molecule. Nevertheless, we would like to note that despite different sensitivities of all parameters, the optimization directions of them are still qualitatively toward the target values. This can also be demonstrated by the transferability of the refined force field to reproduce the experimental pressure−temperature phase diagram and isobaric heat capacity, as discussed in Supporting Information Subsection S4.3. Such an example shows that analyzing the sensitivity of force field parameters offers valuable insights into the underlying interactions of the material system. We further realize that the density distribution from the MBAR reweighted sampling scheme typically has peaks with a slightly asymmetric feature under the phase coexistence condition, so this asymmetry may not be well captured by the symmetric Gaussian distribution. Skewed Gaussian distribution could be employed to address this limitation in the future. Another potential drawback is that multiple force field parameters in one type of potential energy function are sometimes interrelated when predicting a single property (e.g., the gas−liquid phase diagram in this work), which results in difficulty of independent refinement for these multiple parameters. This challenge could be resolved through refinement with multiobjects (i.e., physical properties) as targets. We will try to extend our developed strategy to automatic refinement of force fields based on solid−liquid and solid− solid phase diagrams in the future. However, the current density-based differentiable loss function cannot be directly transferred to the solid−liquid and solid−solid transition without modification. First, density may not serve as an appropriate order parameter for these two types of phase
[Page 7]

transitions, as the volume fluctuations are often small and the volume change between two phases is not always sufficiently large to reliably distinguish them. Second, unlike gas−liquid coexistence, solid−liquid transitions lack a critical point, where large fluctuations of the physical quantities and diverging correlation lengths of the system could facilitate phase coexistence sampling via the PTMC approach. Facing these two reasons, to extend our refinement strategy to the solid− liquid and solid−solid transition, we envision employing enhanced sampling techniques to perform phase coexistence simulations based on well-established collective variables to distinguish the phases. The differentiable loss function could then be constructed from the distribution of physical quantities such as internal energy or enthalpy for which experimental reference data are available, enabling automatic force field refinement in the context of solid−solid or solid−liquid phase equilibria. There are different simulation methods to study the phase transition, which can be broadly categorized into two main approaches: direct coexistence simulations and free-energybased simulation methods.1 Correspondingly, force field refinement methods can be constructed upon either type of simulation method. Our proposed method refines force fields based on the direct coexistence simulation using the HPTMC method. In contrast, two recent proposed methods of Fuchs et al. (2025)52 and Swinburne et al. (2025)53 exemplify the freeenergy-based route to refine force fields based on solid−liquid and solid−solid transitions, where the differentiable loss function is constructed from the condition that two phases in equilibrium must have equal free energy under the relevant ensemble. For instance, under isothermal−isobaric conditions, the Gibbs free energies of the two phases in equilibrium are equal. Due to this principle, by computing the free energies separately around each phase’s equilibrium structures, these methods circumvent the challenge of sampling rare events associated with high energy barriers between phases. Meanwhile, numerous techniques have been developed to compute free energies accurately, offering advantages over direct coexistence simulations in certain contexts. Accordingly, freeenergy-based automated force field refinement methods possess unique advantages in the context of solid-state phase transitions, relative to our method. However, we also would like to kindly point out that, when free energy is computed separately for each phase, the configurational sampling is typically confined to regions near the respective equilibrium structures, potentially overlooking intermediate configurations that are relevant to the transformation pathway. Moreover, computing free energies of either phase is often a multistep and technically involved process, which could introduce complex dependencies between the loss function and the force field parameters, potentially limiting the universality of refinement methods across different systems. In the work of Fuchs et al. (2025),52 the target free energy differences G I0 II of two phases are approximated by the irreversible work computed from nonequilibrium simulations,54,55 and the free energy variations of pure phases F I/II from force field parameter updates are estimated using a hybrid free energy methods56 along a thermodynamic integration path. The accuracy of nonequilibrium thermodynamic integration depends on the system and simulation settings, which might undermine the reliability of target free energy differences in the loss function. Additionally, for

complex systems with large parameter shifts, statistical errors in hybrid free energy methods may accumulate along longer integration paths, potentially reducing overall robustness. In the work of Swinburne et al. (2025),53 the proposed descriptor density of states (D-DOS) method leverages score matching57 to estimate the entropy of descriptor vectors S( | ) based on an isosurface function from a reference potential. Free energy is then obtained via Laplace’s method58 based on the trained entropy model S ( | ), which is independent of the force field parameters. This method could introduce complexity in its sampling simulations (e.g., on isosurfaces functions) and score matching to estimate the entropy of descriptor vectors, potentially leading to a system-dependent loss function that depends on the force field parameters through intricate statistical mechanical relations, including Legendre dualities and density bridges between reference and target models. Our proposed force field refinement strategy using the direct phase coexistence simulations partially circumvents some of these challenges. Configurations along the transformation pathway are naturally sampled during coexistence simulations, and the loss function is directly constructed from the physical quantity distribution, which is related to the force field parameters via straightforward reweighting factors. These features constitute key advantages of our method. Ultimately, force field refinement methods built upon different simulation methods each carry their own advantages and limitations depending on the specific system under study. Rather than a universally superior approach, the appropriate choice is ultimately the one best suited to the problem at hand. This approach may also be implemented for the refinement of molecular force fields with many more parameters or even MLFFs. In principle, a good physics-based force field can predict a broad range of thermodynamic and kinetic properties with adequate accuracy (as exemplified by an ab initio-based CO 2 force field 59 ). However, the transferability and expressivity of any specific force field inherently depend on its functional form, and we note that the TraPPE model (used here just as a modeling case to assess our phase-diagramtargeted refinement strategy) employs a simple functional form with few parameters and is therefore not expected to achieve high accuracy across all properties, whereas the cutting-edge and rapidly evolving area of MLFFs offers a promising route to greatly enhance force-field quality and expressivity. Regarding the refinement for an MLFF, an initial force field version can be obtained by training with the ab initio data set, and we can then fine-tune the parameters of this MLFF by fitting the investigated material system’s phase diagrams via the top-down framework in this study. Given that the good local minima in the parameter landscape can be located via gradient-based optimization algorithms leveraging gradient computed with automatic differentiation, the primary challenge in extending our refinement strategy to MLFFs may not be the parameter number in the force fields, nor the architectural complexity, but rather the increasing computational cost with the increase of parameter number and the need to verify that the differentiable loss function originally designed for classical force fields remains effective and stable when navigating the highdimensional, nonconvex loss landscapes characteristic of MLFFs. As for the computational cost, taking the L-J system as an example, with a resampling interval of 50 steps and total optimization steps of 800 steps, 16 trajectory resamplings are performed throughout the entire refinement. Each resampling

[Page 8]

■

event involves 2 million (2M) simulation steps per replica, leading to a total of 16 × 2M steps�a simulation cost that is, in principle, feasible for MLFFs, though practically demanding. (The wall-clock time of both systems is exhibited in Supporting Information Subsection S3.4) Ultimately, addressing these challenges will require careful benchmarking and potentially adaptive resampling strategies to balance accuracy and efficiency, which we identify as a promising direction for future work.

ACKNOWLEDGMENTS The authors acknowledge funding support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 92470114, no. 52273223), Ministry of Science and Technology of the People’s Republic of China (grant no. 2021YFB3800303), and DP Technology Corporation (grant no. 2021110016001141). The computing resource of this work was provided by the Bohrium Cloud Platform, which was supported by DP Technology.

5. CONCLUSIONS In summary, we develop a phase-diagram-based force field refinement method leveraging automatic differentiation technique via the DMFF platform, to generate reliable classical force fields for characterizing phases’ coexistence. HPTMC methods are applied to realize sufficient phases’ equilibration sampling. The strategy is validated on the gas−liquid phase diagram, for which a differentiable loss function is designed. The approach yields refined force fields producing phase diagrams closely matching targets, while automatically determining phase-equilibrium chemical potentials. Our proposed methodology thus establishes an extensible paradigm for refining force fields dealing with phase transitions.
